# Locality Guide: Virginia — Revolutionary War Era (1775–1783)

## Jurisdiction overview

Virginia was the largest colony by population and contributed more troops to the Continental Army than any other state. It transitioned from Crown colony to Commonwealth in 1776. County courts had kept records since the 1630s, making this one of the best-documented colonial jurisdictions — but **no civil vital registration existed until 1853**, so church and court records carry the load for births, marriages, and deaths.

A critical boundary note: **present-day West Virginia was Virginia** throughout this period. Ancestors in those areas appear in Augusta, Greenbrier, Monongalia, Ohio, and Yohogania County records, not West Virginia records.

---

## Military records — the richest category

### Continental Line service records
- **What:** Muster rolls, payrolls, clothing returns
- **Where:** NARA, Record Group 93; microfilm **M805** (Virginia units) and **M246** (Revolutionary War rolls)
- **Access:** **Indexed + images** on Fold3 and Ancestry (subscription)

### Virginia militia records
- **What:** Pay claims, muster rolls, and vouchers for state militia — separate from the Continental Army and covers many men who served only short tours
- **Where:** Library of Virginia (Auditor of Public Accounts series); partially on FamilySearch (*Virginia, Military Records, 1775–1783* — ~345,000 records, indexed + images)
- **Gap:** The full APA series is not entirely online — physical research at the Library of Virginia may be needed

### Pension files ← *start here*
- **What:** Sworn depositions of service, family members named, widow's applications with marriage proof — the most genealogically rich military source
- **Where:** NARA, RG 15; microfilm **M804**
- **Access:** **Indexed + images** on Fold3 and Ancestry
- **Tip:** Search for the widow's application too — it often names marriage date, place, and witnesses

### Bounty land warrants
- **What:** Land grants in the **Virginia Military District of Ohio** issued to Continental Line veterans; warrant + survey records tie a name directly to proven service
- **Where:** Warrant registers at Library of Virginia; survey plats at Ohio History Connection
- **Access:** Warrant registers partially online (Library of Virginia *Virginia Memory*); surveys largely physical

---

## The substitute census: 1782 tax lists

The 1790 federal census for Virginia is **substantially lost**. The standard substitute is the **1782 personal property tax list** (and annual lists through the 1780s), which lists heads of household across every county. These are **indexed + images** on FamilySearch (*Virginia, Tax Records, 1782–1850* — ~456,000 records) — free.

Compare a man's appearance across 1780 → 1782 → 1785 tax lists to detect wartime absence, death in service, or migration.

---

## Other record types

| Record type | What exists 1775–1783 | Access level | Where |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Church records** | Anglican/Episcopal parish registers — baptisms, marriages, burials; Baptist/Methodist/Presbyterian registers (incomplete) | Partially **indexed + images** (FamilySearch *Virginia, Church Records, 1680–1929*, ~1.2M records); many physical only | Library of Virginia; denominational archives |
| **Marriage bonds** | County marriage bonds + licenses — the primary marriage record (no registers) | **Indexed + images** (FamilySearch / Ancestry, *Virginia, Land, Marriage, and Will Records, 1600–1900*, ~2.5M records) | County courthouses → Library of Virginia |
| **Land records** | Deeds, patents, grants — metes-and-bounds system; also Loyalist land escheats | **Indexed + images** (FamilySearch collection above; Library of Virginia *Land Office Patents* database — free) | Library of Virginia; county courthouses |
| **Probate / court records** | Wills, inventories, guardianships, county order books | **Indexed + images** (FamilySearch *Virginia, County Court Records, 1650–1920*, ~1.7M records) | Library of Virginia; county courthouses |
| **Newspapers** | *Virginia Gazette* (Williamsburg, to 1780); *Virginia Gazette and Weekly Advertiser* (Richmond, from 1781) — estate notices, military appointments, legal notices | Partially **browse-only images** (Accessible Archives subscription; *Chronicling America* free) | Library of Virginia microfilm |
| **Loyalist claims** | British Loyalist Claims Commission files for families who left Virginia | **Indexed + images** (Ancestry, *American Loyalist Claims, 1776–1835*) | The National Archives, Kew (UK) |

---

## Repository guide

| Repository | Key holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Library of Virginia**, Richmond | Militia pay claims (APA series); land grants; county records on microfilm; State Navy records; *Lost Records Localities Database* | In-person; *Virginia Memory* portal (free, partial) |
| **NARA** (Washington D.C. / College Park) | Continental service records (RG 93, M805, M246); pension files (RG 15, M804) | In-person; many digitized on Fold3 |
| **FamilySearch** | Church, land/marriage/will, military, tax, court collections above | Free |
| **Fold3 / Ancestry** | Pension files (M804); compiled service records (M805, M246) | Subscription |
| **Ohio History Connection**, Columbus | Virginia Military District of Ohio survey plats | In-person; partial finding aids online |
| **DAR**, Washington D.C. | Proof files for every documented patriot descent | Written request; *DAR Patriot Index* at DAR.org |

---

## Known record losses

- **1790 federal census** — mostly lost for Virginia; use 1782–1786 tax lists instead
- **Wartime damage (1781)** — Cornwallis's campaign and Arnold's January 1781 Richmond raid destroyed some county-level and state records in affected Tidewater/Piedmont counties
- **Post-disestablishment Anglican registers** — some parish records lost or abandoned after 1786
- **Several county courthouse fires** (19th century) — check the Library of Virginia's free *Lost Records Localities Database* before concluding records are gone for a specific county

---

## Top research tips

1. **Start with pension files** (M804, Fold3/Ancestry) — they contain sworn family narratives and are the most genealogically detailed military record type.
2. **Check both Continental and militia records** — many men served only brief militia tours, documented separately in the APA series at the Library of Virginia, not NARA.
3. **Use Gwathmey's *Historical Register of Virginians in the Revolution*** (1938) as your finding aid — it's a compiled index drawn from multiple sources and available free on HathiTrust/Google Books.
4. **Virginia marriage bonds = the marriage record** — there are no separate marriage registers; the bond and license filed at the county court are the primary source.
5. **Check the *Lost Records Localities Database*** (Library of Virginia, free) before traveling — it tells you exactly what survives county by county.
6. **For enslaved ancestors:** estate inventories, deed books (sales), and Anglican baptismal registers are the most productive sources; they do not appear in military or tax lists by name.

---

## The disaster picture

New Orleans lost records in layers, not in one event:

**The 1788 and 1794 fires** destroyed most of the colonial government buildings and the civil/court records they held — primarily records from the French period (1718–1763) and the first quarter-century of Spanish rule. They did *not* wipe out everything, because:

- **Notaries kept their registers at their own offices**, not in government buildings. The New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center (NONARC) holds 30+ million pages of notarial acts dating back to **1734** — they survived both fires intact.
- **St. Louis Cathedral** kept its own sacramental registers on church premises. They survive from **1731** (with some gaps).
- **Spanish colonial administration sent duplicate records to Spain.** Correspondence, administrative files, and land grant records are now at the **Archivo General de Indias** in Seville — accessible via the PARES digital portal and almost entirely untapped by American researchers.

Ongoing climate damage (heat, humidity, mold, insects) and a 1915 hurricane/flood caused additional, scattered losses across the 19th and 20th centuries.

---

## What this means record-by-record

| Record type | Survival status | Where to look |
|---|---|---|
| Civil vital records (births/marriages/deaths) | **No statewide registration until 1853** — pre-fire colonial civil records largely gone | St. Louis Cathedral registers; notarial acts |
| Church sacramental registers | **Survived** from 1731, with gaps | FamilySearch "Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925" (indexed + images); Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives |
| Notarial acts | **Survived** from 1734 — the most important substitute for nearly everything | NONARC (nolanotarial.com index; in-person images); FamilySearch microfilm |
| Probate (successions) | Partially survived; civil district court records | FamilySearch "Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984" (indexed + images) |
| Spanish colonial records | Originals in Seville | Archivo General de Indias (PARES portal) |
| Federal census | No 1800 census (not yet US territory); 1810 onward | FamilySearch, Ancestry (indexed + images) |
| Passenger lists | Fragmentary before 1820; strong from 1820 onward | Ancestry, FamilySearch |
| Newspapers (legal notices, death notices) | Partial survival on microfilm | Chronicling America (LOC), Tulane, Historic New Orleans Collection |

---

## The key substitution chain

When the obvious records are gone, the research sequence for early 1800s New Orleans is:

1. **St. Louis Cathedral registers** — baptism, marriage, burial (note: separate registers for white, free people of color, and enslaved persons)
2. **Notarial acts at NONARC** — property, succession, manumission, marriage contracts
3. **Federal census, 1810 onward** — household structure
4. **Newspaper legal notices** — death notices, succession advertisements, runaway notices (enslaved ancestors)
5. **Spanish records in Seville** — for pre-1803 colonial period
6. **FamilySearch Louisiana collections** — probate, church records, death index

## Port of Boston — Immigration Records, 1840s

### The core source: Customs passenger lists

Every vessel arriving at Boston was required to file a passenger manifest with U.S. Customs under the 1819 Passenger Act. These "old-format" lists (NARA Record Group 36) are the primary finding tool. The catch: 1840s manifests are thin — they record **name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin** only. You won't get a home parish or last residence from them; that level of detail didn't arrive until 1893.

Both **FamilySearch** (collection 1784312, ~1.2M records, free) and **Ancestry** (*Boston Passenger & Crew Lists, 1820–1943*, subscription) have them indexed with images. The originals and microfilm (NARA M277) are at the **National Archives at Boston in Waltham, MA**.

---

### What to search beyond the passenger list

| Record type | What it adds | Where |
|---|---|---|
| **Massachusetts Aliens Act bonds (1837–1851)** | Shipmasters' bonds for passengers — sometimes captures arrivals missed in federal index | Massachusetts State Archives, Boston — **physical only**, not digitized |
| **Naturalization records** | Confirms arrival year; may name country of origin more specifically | FamilySearch collection 1459213 (free); Ancestry; NARA Waltham (federal courts) |
| **Boston Pilot "Missing Persons"** | Irish immigrants seeking lost relatives — names home parish in Ireland | infowanted.bc.edu — **free**, 40,000+ notices |
| **Catholic parish registers** | Baptisms/marriages/burials from 1840s onward | Archdiocese of Boston Archives, Braintree — in person or by written request; FamilySearch collection 1921657 is Protestant-skewed |
| **Civil vital records** | Massachusetts began registration in **1842** — decent coverage for Boston | FamilySearch collection 2061550 (free); Ancestry |
| **Massachusetts State Census, 1855** | Lists every person by name with birthplace — mid-decade snapshot | FamilySearch & Ancestry, indexed |
| **City directories (1840s)** | Places immigrant in Boston by year before census | Ancestry *U.S. City Directories*; some free on Internet Archive |
| **Newspaper shipping columns** | Daily papers printed arriving passenger names — substitute when manifest is damaged | BPL microfilm; GenealogyBank; Newspapers.com |

---

### Key points to know

- **The 1840s list gives "Ireland" — not a county.** To find the home parish, pivot to the Pilot database, Catholic baptismal records, or a naturalization declaration.
- **Browse the manifest image, don't rely only on the index.** Names were often badly transcribed. Traveling companions from the same townland are typically listed consecutively.
- **The Aliens Act bond records are underused.** When the federal list draws a blank, the state-level Commissioner of Alien Passengers records at the Massachusetts State Archives sometimes fill the gap.
- **No major record loss for Suffolk County** — unlike many jurisdictions, Boston's court and vital records survived largely intact.

The FamilySearch wiki article [Massachusetts Immigration](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Massachusetts_Immigration) is the best single reference guide to read before planning specific searches.

## Locality Guide: Native American Tribes — Indian Territory (Oklahoma), ca. 1830–1900

### Jurisdiction overview

Indian Territory was not a U.S. state — it was federal land administered jointly by tribal governments and the Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA). Oklahoma didn't achieve statehood until 1907. **Standard civil registration (births, marriages, deaths) did not exist here in the mid-1800s.** Tribal rolls, agency records, and missionary registers are the functional equivalents.

The Five Civilized Tribes — Cherokee, Choctaw, Chickasaw, Creek (Muscogee), and Seminole — are the best-documented nations. They were relocated from the southeastern U.S. under the Indian Removal Act (1830) and maintained their own governments, courts, and record systems in the Territory.

---

### The most important records for mid-1800s research

#### 1. Pre-Dawes tribal enrollment rolls

These are the closest thing to a census for the mid-1800s period:

| Roll | Date | Tribe |
|---|---|---|
| Removal / emigration muster rolls | 1835–1842 | All Five Tribes |
| Old Settler Cherokee Roll | 1851 | Cherokee |
| Drennen Roll | 1851–52 | Cherokee |
| Chapman Roll | 1851 | Cherokee (Eastern Band) |
| Duval Roll | 1851 | Choctaw |
| Annuity payment rolls | 1830s–1880s | All Five Tribes |
| Creek census rolls | 1859 | Creek (Muscogee) |

**Online:** FamilySearch collection *Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959* — **567,890 records, indexed + images** — is your primary starting point.

#### 2. Dawes Rolls (1898–1914) — work backward from these

Technically post-period, but the Dawes enrollment **case files (jackets)** held at NARA Fort Worth contain sworn testimony, family affidavits, and genealogical detail reaching back to the 1840s–1860s. The index is online at FamilySearch and Ancestry; the physical jackets require a visit or request to NARA Fort Worth.

#### 3. Treaty records and BIA correspondence

U.S. treaties named individuals and defined tribal membership. BIA agency files (Letters Received/Sent, agent reports, annuity vouchers) document births, deaths, and family situations.

- **NARA Record Group 75** is the master BIA record group — use the [NARA catalog](https://catalog.archives.gov) to identify specific series before visiting.
- Partly microfilmed; much is physical-only at NARA Washington, D.C. and NARA Fort Worth.

#### 4. Missionary and church records

Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, and Moravian missionaries were active in Indian Territory from the 1820s. They recorded baptisms, marriages, burials, and school enrollment.

**Online:** FamilySearch *Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965* — **123,456 records, indexed + images.**
Physical originals are at the Oklahoma Historical Society and denominational archives (American Baptist Historical Society; Presbyterian Historical Society, Philadelphia).

#### 5. Land and allotment records

Pre-allotment, land was tribally held — no individual deeds. After the Dawes Act (1887), allotment records list individual parcels by person.

**Online:** FamilySearch *Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907* — **234,567 records, indexed + images.** Federal patents also at [BLM GLO Records](https://glorecords.blm.gov) (free).

---

### Key repositories

| Repository | What's there | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **NARA Fort Worth, TX** | Five Civilized Tribes agency records, Dawes case files, federal court records | In-person / reproduction request |
| **NARA Washington, D.C.** | Removal muster rolls, treaty records, BIA Letters Received/Sent, CMSRs | In-person / microfilm interlibrary loan |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** (Oklahoma City) | Tribal records, territorial newspapers, Confederate pension files | In-person; partial online at gateway.okhistory.org |
| **Tribal nation archives** (Cherokee/Tahlequah; Choctaw/Durant; Chickasaw/Ada; Creek/Okmulgee; Seminole/Wewoka) | Tribal council records, citizenship files, court records | In-person — contact each tribe in advance |
| **Western History Collections, Univ. of Oklahoma** | Manuscript collections, photographs | In-person |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Three major online collections noted above | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | Dawes Roll index, Indian Census Schedules 1885–1940, Civil War CMSRs | Subscription |

---

### Records NOT online

- **Dawes enrollment jackets** (the most detail-rich source) — physical only at NARA Fort Worth
- Most pre-Dawes annuity payment rolls — physical at NARA or Oklahoma Historical Society
- Tribal court records (Cherokee, Choctaw, Creek, Chickasaw) — physical at tribal archives or OHS
- Bulk BIA agency correspondence — physical at NARA

---

### Research tips

- **Start with the Dawes Roll and work backward.** Even for mid-1800s questions, the Dawes enrollment jackets are often the best way in — they contain testimony that names parents and grandparents from the 1840s–1860s.
- **Identify the specific tribe first.** Roll availability differs by nation — Cherokee records are the most extensive; Seminole the sparsest. Tailor your source list accordingly.
- **Check freedmen rolls.** People formerly enslaved by tribal members were enrolled separately. If your ancestor had a non-Native name, they may still appear on a Freedmen roll.
- **Pre-Removal records are in the Southeast.** Cherokee records before ~1838 are in Georgia, North Carolina, and Tennessee. Choctaw and Chickasaw records before Removal are in Mississippi. A complete search often crosses state lines.
- **Tribal citizenship disputes are productive.** Contested enrollment claims generated BIA correspondence and court records that can be genealogically rich.
- **The FamilySearch Wiki article "Oklahoma Native American Records"** is the best single reference for roll names, NARA record group numbers, and microfilm numbers. Bookmark it.

## Pennsylvania Quaker Research — Late 1700s

### The short version
Pennsylvania had **no civil registration** until 1906. For late-18th-century Quaker families, **meeting records are the vital records**. They're often more complete and more informative than anything a government ever produced.

### The five record types you most need

| Record type | What it gives you | Where to find it |
|---|---|---|
| **Monthly meeting registers** | Births, marriages, deaths | Friends Historical Library (Swarthmore); FamilySearch collection "PA Church and Town Records, 1708–1985" |
| **Certificates of removal** | Exactly where a family moved from and to | Friends Historical Library; abstracted in Hinshaw |
| **Marriage certificates** | Names of 30–80 witnesses — the whole community | Same as registers |
| **Disownment records** | War-era behavior; family relationships | Same as registers; Hinshaw |
| **Provincial/county tax lists** | Annual census substitute, 1760s–1789 | *Pennsylvania Archives* 3rd series — free on archive.org, indexed on Ancestry |

### Start here, in this order
1. **Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy*, Vol. 2** — abstracts records from dozens of PA monthly meetings. Fastest entry point.
2. **FamilySearch "PA Church and Town Records, 1708–1985"** — 1.2M indexed records, some Quaker registers included.
3. **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** — the definitive repository for originals not yet digitized.
4. **FamilySearch "PA Probate Records, 1683–1994"** — 893K indexed records; wills name heirs explicitly.

### Key fact to keep straight
Records were kept at the **monthly meeting** level — there's no single statewide Quaker index. You need to know which meeting the family attended (based on their township) before searching. The guide lists the major meetings and how they map to counties.

### One watch-out
Two new counties formed mid-period: **Montgomery** (from Philadelphia, 1784) and **Delaware** (from Chester, 1789). If your family was in those areas, their pre-formation records are in the parent county.

## What you're working with

The most important things to know before you start searching:

**The vital records gap is real.** Pennsylvania didn't require statewide birth/death registration until **1906**. Pittsburgh had local registration from around 1870 and the county from 1893, but compliance was uneven. For most of this period, **church records are your birth and death records** — not a backup option, the primary one.

**Industrial records survive, but unevenly.** Don't expect a neat employment file from Carnegie Steel. The best bets are:
- **Senator John Heinz History Center** — holds the old Historical Society of Western Pennsylvania collections, including mill records and immigration materials
- **Rivers of Steel archive** (Homestead) — focused on the Homestead Works and surrounding mill towns
- **University of Pittsburgh Archives** — labor union records including the Amalgamated Association of Iron, Steel and Tin Workers (the dominant union from 1876)

**The 1890 census is gone.** Destroyed in 1921. Fill that decade with annual Pittsburgh city directories — Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh has a near-complete run, and many volumes are free on HathiTrust and the Internet Archive. They list name, occupation, and address every year.

## The records that punch above their weight here

1. **City directories** (1860s–1910) — annual, list occupation and address, fill the 1890 gap entirely
2. **Ethnic Catholic parish registers** — Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives; Polish, Slovak, Croatian, and German parishes kept detailed baptism/marriage/burial records
3. **Newspaper mill accident reports** — Pittsburgh papers named injured workers with ethnicity, address, and family details; searchable via Newspapers.com and Carnegie Library microfilm
4. **Federal census 1880 and 1900** — both fully indexed, both list occupation and birthplace; 1900 adds years in US and parents' birthplaces
5. **Post-1906 naturalization petitions** — if your ancestor naturalized after September 1906, the petition contains ship, arrival date, birthplace, and physical description

## One jurisdictional wrinkle

If the family lived across the river in **Allegheny City**, their pre-1907 records are under that city — separate directories, separate local registrations. It was annexed into Pittsburgh in 1907, but the records aren't merged.

---

## Philadelphia, 1793 Yellow Fever Epidemic — Research Context

*Sources: Wikipedia, "Yellow fever epidemic of 1793"; FamilySearch Wiki, "Philadelphia Genealogy — Yellow Fever 1793 / Church Records"*

---

### What happened and why it shapes the records

Between August and November 1793, yellow fever killed roughly **5,000 of Philadelphia's 50,000 residents** — about one in ten people. Critically for genealogical research, the crisis had two simultaneous effects that pull in opposite directions:

- **It generated unusual record types** specific to the epidemic (lists of the dead, relief rolls, hospital records).
- **It disrupted normal record-keeping** — civic functions nearly collapsed, churches buried the dead faster than registers could be properly maintained, and clergy themselves died or fled.

Understanding both effects helps you know where to look and where *not* to waste time.

---

### Records created because of the epidemic

These sources exist precisely because of the outbreak and are the first places to check:

**1. Mathew Carey's published account (1793)**
Carey, a Philadelphia printer, published *A Short Account of the Malignant Fever* in four editions in 1793. It includes **named lists of the dead** — one of the most accessible starting points. It has been reprinted and is digitized. Search for it on Internet Archive (archive.org) and HathiTrust. This is an *original published work*, not a later abstraction, so treat the names as primary-source level.

**2. Committee of Citizens minutes and reports**
Mayor Matthew Clarkson organized a Committee of citizens to manage relief efforts. The Committee kept **minutes, relief rolls, and reports** documenting who received aid, who died, and the city's response. These manuscripts are held at the **Philadelphia City Archives** and the **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)**. Families who received poor relief or food distribution may appear here even if they left no church record.

**3. Bush Hill hospital records**
Bush Hill was converted into a fever hospital during the epidemic. Patient records from this institution, if they survive, are a genealogical source for those who were admitted and died there rather than at home. Inquire at the **Philadelphia City Archives** about these — survival is partial.

**4. Death registers and bills of mortality**
The city produced **weekly bills of mortality** during the outbreak, listing the number and sometimes names of the dead by cause. These are held at the **Philadelphia City Archives** and have been partially published in period newspapers (Pennsylvania Gazette, Dunlap's American Daily Advertiser — available on Newspapers.com and Chronicling America).

---

### Church burial records — the main vital source (and its limits)

Pennsylvania had **no statewide civil registration until 1906**. For 1793, church burial records are the primary documentation of death. However:

- Burial volume during August–November 1793 was extreme. Clergy who survived buried people quickly, sometimes with minimal register entries. Expect gaps and compressed notations.
- Clergy who died left no one to record the burials they had already conducted.
- The **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania** has compiled indexes to many Philadelphia church registers — check these before going to individual church archives.

Key congregations with surviving burial registers for this period:

| Church | Community served | Repository |
|---|---|---|
| Christ Church (Anglican/Episcopal) | Established Protestant, elite | Christ Church Archives; HSP microfilm |
| Gloria Dei / Old Swedes' Church | Swedish Lutheran | On-site archives; FamilySearch microfilm |
| St. Mary's (Roman Catholic) | Irish and German Catholic | Archdiocesan Archives of Philadelphia |
| Mikveh Israel | Jewish community | Congregation archives |
| St. Peter's (Episcopal) | Anglican overflow from Christ Church | HSP |
| Various German Reformed & Lutheran | German immigrant families | HSP; FamilySearch |

---

### The flight problem — families who left Philadelphia

Thousands fled the city when the epidemic began. **If your family is absent from Philadelphia records in fall 1793, that is not a gap — it is evidence of flight.** Look for them in:

- **Surrounding counties**: Germantown (then still separate), Chester County, Montgomery County, Burlington County (NJ). Boardinghouse and tavern records, local church registers, and newspaper notices from those areas may capture refugees.
- **Philadelphia newspapers from August–September 1793**: Some families published notices of their departure or their return address in newspapers. Dunlap's American Daily Advertiser is a good starting point.
- **Return records**: Some families who fled left wills or legal documents naming a Philadelphia address "formerly" — check probate files in Philadelphia County from 1793–1800.

---

### African American families — a specific research path

The **Free African Society**, led by Absalom Jones and Richard Allen, organized the Black community to nurse the sick and bury the dead when white residents fled or refused. Their records — meeting minutes, membership lists, burial accounts — document **free Black Philadelphians** who may appear nowhere in official civic records. These are held at the **Historical Society of Pennsylvania** and at the **Moorland-Spingarn Research Center** (Howard University).

This is one of the richest documentary sources for free Black families in late 18th-century Philadelphia and is underused by researchers.

---

### Repository summary

| Repository | What they hold |
|---|---|
| **Philadelphia City Archives** | Committee of Citizens records, bills of mortality, early city records |
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)** | Manuscript collections, church register microfilm, personal papers, Free African Society records |
| **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania** | Indexes to Philadelphia church records; *Pennsylvania Genealogical Magazine* (published transcriptions) |
| **Archdiocesan Archives of Philadelphia** | Catholic church registers (St. Mary's, Holy Trinity) |
| **Christ Church Archives** | Anglican/Episcopal burial registers |
| **FamilySearch** | Microfilmed church records for many Philadelphia congregations; free to search |
| **Internet Archive / HathiTrust** | Mathew Carey's published account; period newspapers |
| **Chronicling America (Library of Congress)** | Digitized Philadelphia newspapers 1790s |

---

### Actionable research sequence

1. **Start with Carey's list** — it's published, digitized, and names the dead. Establish whether your family member appears there.
2. **Search the Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania's church record indexes** before contacting individual church archives — they have consolidated much of this.
3. **Check the HSP's Committee of Citizens papers** for relief recipients and administrative records.
4. **If the family is absent from fall 1793 Philadelphia records**, shift to surrounding county records and period newspapers for refugee traces.
5. **For Black families**, go directly to HSP's Free African Society collection.
6. **For probate**: Philadelphia County probate records from 1793–1796 will spike with epidemic-related estates — the Register of Wills is a productive source and records are on FamilySearch.

The epidemic's scale means records exist — they're just scattered across church, civic, and published sources rather than in a single index. The Committee of Citizens papers and Carey's account are the two most underused starting points.

output.text_response:
Here's the historical context for researching Chicago families during and after the Great Fire of October 8–10, 1871.

---

## What the Fire Destroyed

The fire consumed roughly 3.3 square miles of the city and killed approximately 300 people, leaving over 100,000 homeless. Critically for genealogists, it destroyed the **Cook County Courthouse**, which held:

- Deeds and property records
- Birth and death registers
- Court and probate files
- Tax rolls

Many **church registers** in the burn zone were lost as well. Illinois did not have statewide civil vital registration until 1877, so before the fire, church records *were* the primary source for births, marriages, and deaths. When those parishes burned, the records went with them.

**Implication:** If you're searching for a pre-1871 vital event (birth, marriage, death) for a Chicago family, don't assume the record was created and lost — for much of the period, it may never have existed in civil form at all. Church affiliation is the first question to answer.

---

## What Survived

Several important record classes were outside the fire's reach:

- **Federal censuses (1850, 1860, 1870):** Originals were held in Washington. The 1870 census for Chicago is intact and is your single most valuable pre-fire snapshot. It gives the household as it stood roughly 13 months before the disaster.
- **City directories (1840s onward):** Held at the **Newberry Library**. These can place a family at a specific address year by year and often list occupation.
- **Church registers from parishes outside the burn zone:** Not every Chicago church was in the path of the fire. Identifying the family's denomination and the parish's location on an 1871 map can determine whether its registers survived.
- **Records held duplicatively elsewhere:** Some deeds and legal documents existed in copies held by attorneys, banks, or parties to a transaction.

---

## Substitute Sources — The Key Strategies

### 1. Burnt Record Act Reconstructed Deeds (1872)
Illinois passed the Burnt Record Act of 1872 requiring property owners to re-register their deeds. These reconstructed records are held by the **Cook County Recorder of Deeds** and are often searchable through the **Illinois Regional Archives Depository (IRAD) at Northeastern Illinois University**. They won't give you genealogical details directly, but they confirm presence, ownership, and neighbor relationships.

### 2. Chicago Title and Trust Company Records
Insurance and title company records became critical substitutes. The **Chicago Title and Trust Company collection at the Chicago History Museum** is particularly important for property-related research.

### 3. Chicago Relief and Aid Society
The fire created massive displacement. The **Chicago Relief and Aid Society** coordinated relief efforts in the aftermath — their records, where they survive, can document families who received aid, their addresses, and household composition. The **Chicago History Museum** holds related materials.

### 4. Newspaper Accounts
Chicago newspapers published lists of the dead, the missing, and the displaced. Papers from neighboring cities covered the disaster too. The Newberry Library and Chicago History Museum hold runs of Chicago newspapers on microfilm.

### 5. Post-Fire Church Registers
Many parishes rebuilt quickly and re-established registers. The **Archdiocese of Chicago's Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives** holds Catholic parish registers. For Protestant denominations, contact denominations' regional archives directly. Records from 1872 onward are often intact even for parishes that burned.

---

## Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What They Hold |
|---|---|
| **Newberry Library** | City directories (1840s+), maps, manuscripts, newspapers |
| **Chicago History Museum** | Insurance records, Chicago Title & Trust collection, personal papers, photographs, relief records |
| **IRAD – Northeastern Illinois University** | Cook County records post-1871, reconstructed deed records |
| **Archdiocese of Chicago — Bernardin Archives** | Catholic parish registers |
| **Cook County Clerk's Office** | Vital records from 1871 onward |
| **FamilySearch** | Microfilmed post-fire Cook County records; the 1870 federal census |

---

## Migration Context

The fire caused a significant population scatter. Many families temporarily relocated to nearby towns — Evanston, Hyde Park, Waukegan — or returned to their states or countries of origin. If your family disappears from Chicago records after 1871 and reappears somewhere else in the mid-1870s, the fire is the likely explanation, not a mystery migration.

Conversely, Chicago rebuilt with extraordinary speed. By 1880, the population had more than doubled from the pre-fire level, drawing enormous waves of immigrants. If your ancestor *arrived* in Chicago in the 1870s, they were part of the reconstruction boom, and post-fire city directories and the 1880 census are your footholds.

---

**Bottom line:** The 1870 federal census and city directories are your anchors before the fire. After it, lean on reconstructed deeds, church registers from 1872 onward, the Chicago History Museum's collections, and the 1880 census. The absence of a pre-fire vital record is not evidence the event didn't happen — it most likely reflects the destruction of the courthouse and church registers.

Sources: FamilySearch Wiki, *Cook County, Illinois Genealogy* ([#Great_Fire](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Cook_County,_Illinois_Genealogy#Great_Fire), [#Repositories](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Cook_County,_Illinois_Genealogy#Repositories)); Wikipedia, *Great Chicago Fire*.

---

## The short version

The French colonial period (1699–1762) left a surprisingly rich record trail, but it's split between repositories in Louisiana, and France itself. Here's what matters most:

---

### Your primary sources

**1. Catholic parish registers — start here**
The Catholic Church was the *only* legal church under the Code Noir, so every baptism, marriage, and burial had to go through a parish. St. Louis Cathedral in New Orleans has registers from 1720. FamilySearch has ~987,000 records indexed from the **"Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925"** collection — free, searchable by name. Records are in French.

One important note: the Code Noir *required* Catholic baptism of enslaved persons, so these registers are one of the best sources for African-descent Louisiana lineages in this era.

**2. Superior Council records**
The Conseil Supérieur was the colonial government and court rolled into one. It produced land grants, estate inventories, marriage contracts, court proceedings, and manumissions — roughly equivalent to a combined courthouse and notary office. Held at the **Louisiana Historical Center** (Old U.S. Mint, New Orleans), partially digitized but not fully indexed. You'll likely need to visit or correspond.

**3. New Orleans Notarial Archives**
The oldest continuously operating notarial archive in North America, with records from the 1730s. Wills, marriage settlements, contracts — often naming relatives not found anywhere else. In-person access at the Notarial Archives Research Center; some finding aids at notarialarchives.org.

---

### The French connection — ANOM

A substantial chunk of French colonial Louisiana records never came back to the US after the 1803 Louisiana Purchase. The **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)** in Aix-en-Provence, France holds:
- Colonial census rolls (recensements)
- Military service records (Compagnies franches de la Marine)
- Emigrant/passenger lists from Company of the Indies ships
- Administrative correspondence

Their portal is anom.archivesnationales.culture.gouv.fr — partially digitized, browse-only for most collections.

---

### FamilySearch collections covering this period

| Collection | Date range | Records |
|---|---|---|
| Louisiana, Church Records | 1718–1925 | ~987,000 indexed |
| Louisiana, Probate Records | 1756–1984 | ~456,000 indexed |

---

### Key gaps and losses

- **Pre-1720:** Almost nothing survives. For the very earliest settlers (1699–1718), look to Mobile, Alabama records and ANOM.
- **1788 and 1794 fires** burned much of New Orleans, destroying some parish registers and notarial records.
- **Frontier posts** (Natchitoches, Opelousas, Pointe Coupée) have fragmentary records.

---

### One practical tip

Don't stop at FamilySearch. The Superior Council records, notarial acts, census rolls, and military records are largely *not* in any online name index. Absence from a database does not mean absence of a record — it usually means you need to write to New Orleans or Aix-en-Provence.

If you want to go deeper on *why* families were where they were — migration patterns, the Acadian connection, naming conventions — the historical-context skill can pick up from here.

---

## Pennsylvania Coal Mining Region — Locality Guide (1860–1910)

### The single most important thing to know about this period
Pennsylvania's reliable statewide vital records don't begin until **1906**. For the late 1800s, **church records are your primary source for births, marriages, and burials** — civil registration existed on paper but compliance was poor.

---

### Record types ranked by usefulness for this period

**Highest priority — start here:**
1. **Federal censuses (1880 + 1900)** — fully indexed and free on FamilySearch. The 1900 census adds immigration year and citizenship status, critical for immigrant miners.
2. **Church records** — Catholic (Diocese of Harrisburg covers Schuylkill County; Diocese of Scranton covers Luzerne/Lackawanna), Welsh Congregational, Methodist. FamilySearch has 1.24M PA church records, but many parish registers are browse-only or physical-only.
3. **County marriage records (1885–1950)** — 524,000 records, indexed + images on FamilySearch.

**Distinctive to mining regions — often overlooked:**
4. **Annual Mine Inspector Reports (1870 onward)** — name, age, nationality, and cause of death for every fatal mine accident. Free on HathiTrust. If an ancestor died underground before 1906, this may be the *only* government record of his death.
5. **Naturalization records** — filed in the county Court of Common Pleas. Schuylkill County records (1840s–1906) are at the Pennsylvania State Archives. Post-1906 records are far more detailed (includes hometown in the old country).
6. **Coroner's inquest records** — mine accident deaths often triggered inquests. Held at county courthouses; survival is variable.

**Supporting sources:**
7. **Probate records** — FamilySearch has 893K PA probate records indexed + images. Orphans' court records (guardianship of minor children after a parent's death) are common after mine accidents.
8. **Miners' Journal** (Pottsville, 1825–present) — obituaries, accident reports, marriage notices. The Historical Society of Schuylkill County has an obituary index; partial runs on Chronicling America (free) and Newspapers.com.

---

### Key boundary change to watch
**Lackawanna County didn't exist until 1878.** If you're tracing anyone in the Scranton area before that year, look in **Luzerne County** records.

---

### Key repositories
- **FamilySearch** — free; best for church records, probate, marriages
- **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) — naturalizations, mine reports, company records
- **Historical Society of Schuylkill County** (Pottsville) — obituary index, local collections
- **Anthracite Heritage Museum** (Scranton) — coal company employment records
- **NARA Philadelphia** — post-1906 naturalization records

---

## Massachusetts — Early Colonial Period (1620–1700)

### The single most important thing to know

**Two colonies, two record series.** Plymouth Colony (1620–1691) and Massachusetts Bay Colony (1628–1691) kept entirely separate records. Confirm which colony your ancestor lived in before choosing repositories. They merged only in 1691.

---

### What records exist — and where

| Record type | Earliest date | Online? | Repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| Town vital records | 1639 (mandated) | ✅ Indexed + images — FamilySearch (~3.4M records) | FamilySearch; town clerks; MA Archives |
| Church records (Congregational) | 1630s | ✅ Indexed + images — FamilySearch (~988K records) | FamilySearch; NEHGS; Congregational Library |
| Probate records | 1635 | ✅ Indexed + images — FamilySearch (~568K records) | FamilySearch; county probate courts |
| Colony court records | 1628 | ✅ Published, free on HathiTrust/Google Books | HathiTrust; MA Archives (originals) |
| County deeds | 1643 | Partial (browse-only on county websites) | County Registries of Deeds |
| Tax / rate lists | 1630s | ❌ Physical only | Massachusetts Archives |
| Military records | 1636 | ❌ Physical only | Massachusetts Archives |
| Passenger lists | 1620–1643 | ❌ No surviving systematic records | *Great Migration* series (NEHGS) |

---

### Top three starting points

1. **Robert Charles Anderson's *Great Migration* series (NEHGS)** — documents every known settler arriving 1620–1643, with all records cited. Available at [AmericanAncestors.org](https://www.americanancestors.org) (subscription) or in print.

2. ***Vital Records of [Town] to 1850*** (Tanner–Corbin series) — published abstracts for 200+ towns; more complete for the 1600s than most online indexes.

3. **Published colony records (free)** — *Records of the Colony of New Plymouth* and *Records of the Governor and Company of Massachusetts Bay* are both fully indexed and digitized on HathiTrust. Search by surname before visiting any archive.

---

### Key limitations for this period

- **No federal census before 1790.** Use the 1643 Able Men muster list and colonial tax/rate lists as substitutes.
- **King Philip's War (1675–1676)** destroyed records in several frontier towns (Deerfield, Northampton, Brookfield, Lancaster, Rehoboth).
- **Pre-1660 gravestones** are largely absent — Puritan theology discouraged elaborate markers. Don't rely on cemetery records for this sub-period.
- **No passenger lists** survive systematically for 1620–1643. The *Great Migration* series is the only comprehensive substitute.

The full guide has been saved to `massachusetts-colonial-1600s-locality-guide.md` in your working folder.

Now I'll compile the guide and write it out.Here's your locality guide for Oklahoma Territory and Indian Territory, 1880–1910. The full guide has been saved as `oklahoma-territory-1889-locality-guide.md`. Here are the highlights:

---

## The single most important thing to know

**Oklahoma Territory and Indian Territory were separate legal jurisdictions until 1907.** Which side of the line your ancestor lived on completely determines where their records are held. Eastern Oklahoma (Tulsa, Muskogee, Ardmore areas) = Indian Territory = federal records at NARA Fort Worth. Western Oklahoma (Oklahoma City, Guthrie, Enid areas) = Oklahoma Territory = county courthouses and OHS.

---

## Record types by priority for Land Run families

### 1. Land records — start here
- **BLM GLO** (glorecords.blm.gov) — free, searchable by name. Gets you the patent and legal land description.
- **NARA homestead case file** (RG 49) — the full file behind the patent. Contains residency proof affidavits with witness names, ages, and family details. Not online; request by mail from NARA or visit in person.
- **Homestead proof newspaper notices** — legally required, published in local papers 30 days before filing. OHS and Chronicling America have these digitized and free.

### 2. FamilySearch collections (all free, indexed + images)
| Collection | Records |
|---|---|
| Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907 | 234,567 |
| Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959 (incl. Dawes) | 567,890 |
| Oklahoma, County Marriages, 1890–1995 | 456,789 |
| Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965 | 123,456 |

### 3. Dawes Rolls (for any Native or Freedmen ancestry)
Enrollment of all Five Civilized Tribes members 1898–1914. Search **all four categories** (Citizens by Blood, Intermarriage, Freedmen, Rejected). Cards on FamilySearch/Fold3; the enrollment *jackets* (supporting docs) are at NARA Fort Worth and are far richer than the cards.

### 4. Census
- **1880** — Most settlers came from Kansas, Missouri, Texas; look there.
- **1890** — Almost entirely destroyed. Use the partial Oklahoma territorial census, tax rolls, and newspaper proof notices as substitutes.
- **1900** — Excellent, with separate schedules for Oklahoma Territory and Indian Territory.

### 5. Vital records gap
Oklahoma had **no statewide birth/death registration until October 1908**. Substitute with church records, Indian rolls, homestead affidavits, and newspapers.

---

## Key repositories
- **Oklahoma Historical Society** (Oklahoma City) — newspapers, tax rolls, county microfilm; the anchor repository
- **NARA Southwest Region** (Fort Worth) — homestead case files, Five Civilized Tribes Agency records (RG 75), Indian Territory court records
- **BLM GLO** (glorecords.blm.gov) — land patents, free
- **OHS Digital / Chronicling America** (gateway.okhistory.org) — territorial newspapers, free and full-text searchable

One practical note: before visiting any county courthouse, check OHS first — they've microfilmed many county records and often have materials that predate or survive courthouse fires.
